title: Replace ZooKeeper with a Self-Managed Metadata Quorum
summary: Removes Kafka's dependency on ZooKeeper by storing all cluster metadata in an internal Raft-based quorum run by the controllers themselves.
motivation: ZooKeeper is a second distributed system operators must deploy, secure, monitor and tune. It doubles the operational surface and forces a separate security and configuration model on top of Kafka's own.

Metadata scalability is bounded by ZooKeeper. On controller failover the new controller must load the full cluster state from ZooKeeper, and propagate it broker-by-broker — an O(partitions) operation that caps practical partition counts and lengthens recovery.
design: Metadata is stored in a single-partition internal topic, __cluster_metadata, replicated by a Raft quorum. The active controller is the quorum leader; other controllers are hot standbys that already have the full log.

Brokers stop watching ZooKeeper and instead consume the metadata log as an ordered event stream, caching state locally. Failover becomes a matter of a standby taking over leadership with the log already in memory — near-instant rather than an O(partitions) reload.
pros: One system to deploy, secure and operate instead of two.
Controller failover is near-instant; recovery no longer scales with partition count.
Supports clusters with millions of partitions.
Single, consistent security and RBAC model.
cons: A brand-new consensus implementation to harden and battle-test.
Multi-release migration path for existing ZooKeeper clusters.
Operators must relearn tooling, metrics and failure modes.

rejected.name: Keep ZooKeeper but sharded
rejected.why: Sharding metadata across multiple ZK ensembles would reduce Kafka's control over consistency and add even more operational moving parts.

rejected.name: Use an external Raft library
rejected.why: An off-the-shelf library couldn't be tuned to Kafka's log semantics and record format; a purpose-built quorum integrates with the existing replication machinery.

discussion.author: Colin McCabe
discussion.text: This KIP proposes to replace ZooKeeper with a self-managed metadata quorum. It is an umbrella that several follow-up KIPs (the Raft protocol, the controller, migration) will fill in.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: Overall +1 on the direction. My main question is around the migration story — we need a path where a running production cluster can move off ZK without downtime.

discussion.author: Ismael Juma
discussion.text: Agree this is worth doing. Suggest we clearly scope which pieces land in which release so the community can follow the incremental KIPs.

title: Kafka Tiered Storage
authors: Satish Duggana, Harsha Chintalapani, Suresh Srinivas
summary: Decouples storage from compute by offloading older log segments to a cheaper remote store (e.g. object storage) while keeping recent data on the broker's local disk.
motivation: Retaining data for weeks or months forces operators to over-provision broker local disk, coupling storage capacity to broker count and making scaling expensive and slow.
Most reads target recent data; historical segments sit idle on premium local disk. Moving them to cheap remote storage cuts cost and lets brokers scale on throughput, not retention.
design: Each log is split into a local tier (recent segments) and a remote tier. A pluggable RemoteStorageManager uploads closed segments and their indexes to the remote store; a RemoteLogMetadataManager tracks segment locations.
Fetch requests for offsets that have aged out of local storage are served transparently from the remote tier, so consumers see one continuous log regardless of where a segment physically lives.
pros: Local disk sized for recent data only — dramatically lower storage cost.
Retention becomes near-unlimited without growing the broker fleet.
Faster broker recovery and reassignment: less local data to move.
cons: Reads of cold data pay remote-storage latency.
Adds a remote store as a new dependency and failure domain.
Pluggable RSM/RLMM implementations must be correct and performant.

rejected.name: External tiering via a sidecar
rejected.why: Copying segments out-of-band leaves Kafka unaware of remote data, so consumers couldn't transparently read aged-out offsets through the normal fetch path.

rejected.name: Compaction-only retention
rejected.why: Compaction addresses key-based dedup, not the cost of retaining a long history of all records; it doesn't solve the storage-scaling problem.

discussion.author: Harsha Chintalapani
discussion.text: Proposing tiered storage so retention is no longer bound by local disk. The RemoteStorageManager interface is intentionally pluggable so operators can target HDFS, S3, or others.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: We should be explicit about the read path for aged-out segments and the consistency guarantees of the remote metadata store. Correctness there is the crux.

discussion.author: Satish Duggana
discussion.text: Updated the KIP with the RemoteLogMetadataManager design and a topic-based default implementation for tracking remote segment metadata.

title: Exactly Once Delivery and Transactional Messaging
authors: Apurva Mehta, Jason Gustafson, Guozhang Wang, Flavio Junqueira
summary: Introduces idempotent producers and multi-partition atomic writes, giving Kafka exactly-once semantics for produce and read-process-write pipelines.
motivation: The default producer offered at-least-once delivery: a retried send after a network hiccup could produce duplicates, forcing every downstream consumer to dedupe.
Stream-processing apps read, transform, and write back to Kafka. Without atomic writes across output partitions plus offset commits, a crash mid-flight leaves partial, inconsistent results.
design: Idempotence: each producer gets a Producer ID and per-partition sequence numbers, so the broker can detect and drop duplicate retries within a session.

Transactions: a producer wraps writes to multiple partitions — and its consumer offset commits — in a transaction coordinated by a transaction log. Consumers with read_committed only see records once the transaction commits.
pros: True exactly-once for read-process-write topologies.
Idempotence removes duplicates from producer retries with minimal overhead.
Enables correct, atomic Kafka Streams state and output.
cons: Transactions add coordinator state and two-phase commit latency.
read_committed consumers may wait on the Last Stable Offset.
More client and broker complexity to reason about and operate.

rejected.name: Consumer-side dedup only
rejected.why: Pushing dedup to every consumer duplicates effort, is error-prone, and can't make multi-partition writes atomic.

rejected.name: Per-message unique keys
rejected.why: Application-level dedup keys don't cover offset commits and cross-partition atomicity, so they can't deliver end-to-end exactly-once.

discussion.author: Apurva Mehta
discussion.text: This KIP covers idempotent produce and transactions. Idempotence ships first and is cheap; transactions build on the same Producer ID machinery.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: The Last Stable Offset is key: read_committed consumers must not see records past an open transaction, or we break isolation.

discussion.author: Jay Kreps
discussion.text: Big +1. This is foundational for stream processing correctness. Worth being very careful about the default configs so most users get idempotence without surprises.

title: The Next Generation of the Consumer Rebalance Protocol
summary: Replaces the client-driven, stop-the-world rebalance with a broker-coordinated, incremental protocol that removes global synchronization barriers.
motivation: The classic protocol relies on a global synchronization barrier: every member must rejoin and the whole group stalls on each membership change, causing latency spikes and cascading rebalances at scale.
Assignment logic lived in the clients, so fixing bugs or changing strategy meant upgrading every consumer — painful across polyglot fleets.
design: The group coordinator on the broker computes assignments and drives reconciliation. Members send their current state via a heartbeat and receive incremental target assignments.
Reconciliation is incremental: only the partitions that actually move are revoked and reassigned, and members that are unaffected keep processing — no group-wide stop-the-world.
pros: No stop-the-world; unaffected members keep consuming during a rebalance.
Assignment logic on the broker — upgrade once, not every client.
Scales to much larger consumer groups with less disruption.
cons: New protocol coexisting with the old one during a long migration window.
More coordinator-side CPU and state.
Client and tooling ecosystem must add support incrementally.

rejected.name: Incrementally patch the old protocol
rejected.why: Cooperative rebalancing (KIP-429) already softened revocation, but the global JoinGroup barrier is structural and can't be removed without a new protocol.

rejected.name: Fully client-side redesign
rejected.why: Keeping assignment in clients perpetuates the upgrade-everyone problem and can't leverage the coordinator's global view.

discussion.author: David Jacot
discussion.text: Proposing a server-driven rebalance protocol. The core idea: move the assignor to the coordinator and make reconciliation incremental via heartbeats.

discussion.author: Guozhang Wang
discussion.text: Strong support. The upgrade path from the old protocol needs to be crystal clear since both will run in the same group for a while.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: The incremental reconciliation state machine is the tricky part — we should document the member and coordinator states exhaustively.

title: Producer will Enable the Strongest Delivery Guarantee by Default
summary: Turns on producer idempotence by default (enable.idempotence=true, acks=all) so out-of-the-box producers get exactly-once-in-order delivery per partition.
motivation: Idempotence existed since 0.11 but was off by default, so most producers silently ran at-least-once and could reorder or duplicate records on retry.
Safe defaults matter: the majority of users never tune these configs, and they deserve the strongest guarantee Kafka can give without extra work.
design: Default enable.idempotence to true, which implies acks=all and a bounded max.in.flight.requests.per.connection, preserving ordering under retries.
If a user sets configs that conflict with idempotence (e.g. acks=1), the producer either overrides sensibly or fails fast with a clear message rather than silently degrading.
pros: No duplicates or reordering on retry, for free, for most users.
Aligns the default behavior with what users generally expect.
Negligible throughput cost on modern brokers.
cons: acks=all can slightly raise latency versus acks=1.
Existing configs that assumed the old defaults may need review.
Edge-case config conflicts must be handled gracefully.

rejected.name: Keep idempotence opt-in
rejected.why: Leaving it off means most producers never benefit; safe-by-default is the whole point of the change.

rejected.name: Enable idempotence but keep acks=1
rejected.why: acks=1 can't guarantee no data loss on leader failover, undermining the strongest-guarantee goal.

discussion.author: Cheng Tan
discussion.text: Proposing to flip enable.idempotence to true by default. The main discussion point is how to handle users who explicitly set conflicting configs.

discussion.author: Ismael Juma
discussion.text: +1. We should fail fast on genuine conflicts rather than silently overriding, so users aren't surprised by behavior changes.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: Agreed. Let's make sure the error messages point users to exactly which config to change.

title: Alter Replication Protocol to use Leader Epoch for Truncation
summary: Uses a monotonically increasing leader epoch to decide log truncation on recovery, fixing data-loss and divergence edge cases in the old high-watermark scheme.
motivation: The old truncation logic relied only on the high watermark. After certain leader-change and crash sequences, a returning replica could truncate to the wrong offset, causing silent log divergence or data loss.
The high watermark alone can't distinguish records from different leadership terms, so a follower had no reliable way to know exactly where its log diverged from the leader's.
design: Each leader stamps its records with a leader epoch and maintains a leader-epoch-to-start-offset map. On becoming follower, a replica asks the leader for the end offset of its last known epoch.
The follower truncates precisely to that offset — the true point of divergence — instead of guessing from the high watermark, guaranteeing logs converge correctly.
pros: Eliminates a class of silent data-loss and divergence bugs.
Truncation is precise and provably correct across leader changes.
Foundation for later correctness work in the replication layer.
cons: Adds a leader-epoch cache and a new inter-broker request.
On-disk and protocol format changes require careful upgrade handling.

rejected.name: Tune high-watermark timing
rejected.why: No amount of timing tweaks fixes the fundamental inability of the high watermark to identify the divergence point across epochs.

discussion.author: Ben Stopford
discussion.text: The high-watermark-based truncation has genuine correctness gaps. Leader epochs give us an unambiguous divergence point for follower truncation.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: +1 on using leader epochs. We need to be careful about the upgrade path since this changes the on-disk format and adds a new request type.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: This closes real bugs we've seen in the field. Let's document the exact follower recovery sequence step by step.

title: Introduce Incremental FetchRequests to Increase Partition Scalability
summary: Replaces full fetch requests with a stateful, incremental session so brokers and consumers only exchange data for partitions that actually changed.
motivation: Every fetch request listed every partition and its offset, even for idle partitions. With thousands of partitions the request/response overhead dominated, wasting network and CPU.
This overhead scaled with total partition count rather than active partitions, capping how many partitions a broker or consumer could follow efficiently.
design: A fetch session is established once; the broker tracks the client's per-partition state under a session ID. Subsequent incremental fetches send only partitions that changed since the last round.
Idle partitions are omitted entirely, so steady-state fetch traffic scales with the number of active partitions, not the total.
pros: Fetch overhead scales with active, not total, partitions.
Substantially higher partition density per broker and consumer.
Less network and CPU for large, mostly-idle topics.
cons: Brokers must maintain per-session state and evict stale sessions.
More complex fetch protocol and error/recovery handling.

rejected.name: Compress the full fetch request
rejected.why: Compression shrinks bytes but not the per-partition bookkeeping and CPU cost; it doesn't change the O(total partitions) scaling.

discussion.author: Colin McCabe
discussion.text: Proposing incremental fetch sessions so we stop re-sending state for partitions that haven't changed. This is the main blocker for very high partition counts per broker.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: +1. Please spell out the session cache eviction policy — we don't want unbounded broker memory from abandoned sessions.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: The incremental protocol looks right. Make sure clients can gracefully fall back to a full fetch if their session is evicted.

title: Allow Consumers to Fetch from Closest Replica
summary: Lets consumers read from a nearby follower instead of always the leader, cutting cross-datacenter and cross-AZ network cost and latency.
motivation: Consumers historically had to fetch only from the partition leader. In multi-AZ or multi-region deployments that meant expensive, high-latency cross-zone reads even when a local replica held the data.
Cross-AZ traffic is a major cloud cost line item; forcing all reads through the leader inflates both bill and tail latency.
design: Brokers expose rack/zone metadata and a pluggable replica selector. The consumer sends its own rack id; the broker replies with the preferred read replica for that location.
Followers serve reads only up to the high watermark, preserving consumer consistency, while the leader still handles all writes.
pros: Large cut in cross-AZ / cross-region network cost.
Lower read latency by staying within a zone.
Pluggable selector lets operators encode their own topology.
cons: Follower reads can lag the leader up to the high watermark.
Requires accurate rack/zone configuration to pay off.
Adds replica-selection logic to the fetch path.

rejected.name: Client-side leader avoidance
rejected.why: Without broker-provided topology and high-watermark guarantees, clients can't safely choose a replica without risking inconsistent reads.

discussion.author: Jason Gustafson
discussion.text: Proposing follower fetching driven by a pluggable replica selector. Reads stay bounded by the high watermark so consistency is unchanged.

discussion.author: David Arthur
discussion.text: The rack-aware default selector should cover the common cloud AZ case; operators with exotic topologies can plug in their own.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: +1. Let's be explicit that followers only serve up to the HW so users understand the latency-vs-cost trade-off.

title: Queues for Kafka
summary: Adds share groups — a queue-like consumption model where many consumers cooperatively read a topic without being bound to partition ownership.
motivation: Classic consumer groups tie parallelism to partition count: you can't have more active consumers than partitions, which is awkward for work-queue-style workloads.
Many teams want point-to-point queue semantics — competing consumers, per-message acknowledgement, redelivery — which Kafka's partition-ownership model doesn't naturally provide.
design: A share group lets multiple consumers read from the same partitions cooperatively. Records are delivered to individual consumers and individually acknowledged, rather than being tied to a committed offset per partition.
The broker tracks per-record delivery state (acquired, acknowledged, released) so unacknowledged records can be redelivered, enabling competing-consumer queue behavior on top of the existing log.
pros: Consumer parallelism decoupled from partition count.
Per-message acknowledgement and redelivery — true queue semantics.
Reuses the existing durable log rather than a separate queue system.
cons: Broker must track per-record delivery state — new state and overhead.
Weaker ordering guarantees than partition-ordered consumption.
Still maturing: shipped as early access, semantics may evolve.

rejected.name: A separate queue product
rejected.why: Building queues as a distinct system would fragment Kafka's storage and tooling; layering share groups on the existing log reuses durability and ops.

rejected.name: More partitions for parallelism
rejected.why: Scaling partitions to add consumers is expensive and doesn't give per-message ack or redelivery semantics.

discussion.author: Andrew Schofield
discussion.text: Proposing share groups to bring queue semantics to Kafka: competing consumers, individual acknowledgement, and redelivery on top of the existing log.

discussion.author: Jun Rao
discussion.text: Interesting direction. The per-record delivery state tracking is the crux — we need to bound its memory and be clear about the ordering guarantees users can expect.

discussion.author: David Jacot
discussion.text: +1 to ship as early access first so we can gather real usage feedback before locking the semantics.
